mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in managing configurations.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install ECK.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This is the official repository for ECK.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the ECK operator.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Deploy an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

```

> Creates a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and healthy.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system

kubectl describe elasticsearch my-elasticsearch -n elastic-system

```

> Lists all Elasticsearch clusters in the elastic-system namespace.
> Describes the Elasticsearch cluster to check its status.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all Elasticsearch clusters and data managed by the operator.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system -o yaml > elasticsearch-backup.yaml

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system -o yaml > elasticsearch-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Elasticsearch cluster may be in the process of starting up or has failed to start.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Elasticsearch pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n elastic-system'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Elasticsearch cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient resources allocated to the Kubernetes nodes.

**Fix:**
Ensure that your nodes meet the minimum resource requirements (2 vCPU, 4GB RAM).

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to Elasticsearch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service configurations may be blocking access.

**Fix:**
Check your network policies and ensure that the Elasticsearch service is correctly configured.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Helm install failed: chart not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified Helm chart version may not exist in the repository.

**Fix:**
Verify the chart version and update your Helm repository with 'helm repo update'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.13, ensure your Elasticsearch configurations are compatible with version 9.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'Health: green' when everything is working correctly.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.

description: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the minimum resource requirements.
